source.text:
Tomáš Novák: During the Sev1 we had a pretty big runbook gap for TP hangs. We did the right things eventually (drain/replace replicas + cap concurrency) but we were basically guessing. Can we capture what we *wish* the runbook had?

Rafael Mendes: +1. In incident channel folks kept asking “what do I check to confirm it’s a TP stall vs just slow traffic” and we didn’t have a crisp checklist.

Aisha Bello: Also no single dashboard panel to point at. We bounced between request timeouts, GPU util, and random logs.

Tomáš Novák: Concrete gaps I noticed:
1) What commands to run *immediately* on a suspected wedged TP replica
2) Which metrics confirm heartbeat/collective stall
3) What mitigations are safe (and in what order)
4) When to page runtime vs keep in SRE lane

Noah Patel: Agree. For TP stalls the biggest distinction is “comms/collective progress stopped” vs “scheduler starvation/backpressure causing no progress”. We need both signals.

Hiro Tanaka: From kernels side, we should explicitly say: if NCCL collectives are stuck, restarting the pod is often the only recovery. But we should capture logs *before* restart if possible.

Rafael Mendes: What should oncall run right now (today), given current tooling?

Omar El-Sayed: Minimal triage checklist (current state):
- Identify replica/pod(s) correlated with timeouts.
- Check for TP progress logs / per-rank heartbeat (we don’t have consistent metrics yet, but some logs exist).
- Pull NCCL warnings if enabled.

But yeah… not copy/pasteable.

Jacob Stein: We can at least document a “grab bag” of commands. Something like:

1) Find worst offenders (pods) for timeouts
```
# pick namespace/cluster accordingly
kubectl -n runtime get pods -l app=redwood-runtime -o wide
kubectl -n runtime top pod | head
```
2) Tail runtime logs for stall hints
```
kubectl -n runtime logs -f <pod> -c runtime --since=10m | egrep -i "tp|nccl|collective|watchdog|stall|timeout"
```
3) Quick GPU snapshot (if we have node access / daemonset)
```
# via debug container / node shell
nvidia-smi
nvidia-smi dmon -s pucm -d 2 -c 5
```

We should decide the supported path (we don’t always want node access in runbook).

Aisha Bello: From metrics side (what we can reliably query in Prom right now):
- `request_timeout_total{route=...,model=...}` broken down by `pod`
- `runtime_queue_depth` (coarse)
- `gpu_utilization` / `gpu_memory_used_bytes`

But we’re missing: per-rank heartbeat gap, in-flight collective counters, and “stall reason”. That’s what ENG-4823/PR-21834 are adding, but runbook should mention “if available in >=1.19”.

Sofia Ivanova: Scheduler-specific: when we see long prefill waves, decode can starve and then collectives get out of sync. We need a specific “scheduler starvation suspicion” indicator:
- queue depth growing while GPU util oscillates
- decode iterations not advancing (we might only infer via token output stall)

Noah Patel: Can we standardize the “is it wedged?” test for streaming? During the incident we used “no tokens streamed for >N seconds” but N varied.

Rafael Mendes: For oncall we need a decision tree:
- If customer-facing streaming timeouts increasing AND same pods implicated AND tokens stop streaming -> treat as probable TP stall.
- If GPU util high but tokens slow -> capacity/overload.
- If GPU util low and queue depth high -> scheduler/backpressure.

Tomáš Novák: Mitigations we used (and should document):
1) Drain suspected replicas out of service (so they stop taking requests)
2) Restart pods to clear wedge
3) Temporarily reduce concurrency / batch limits for TP routes
4) If still ongoing: reroute to non-TP or smaller model variant (if policy allows)

Connor O'Brien: From release/ops standpoint, please call out feature flags / config knobs explicitly. During the incident someone asked “what’s the safest knob to turn” and we had to dig.

Hiro Tanaka: Knobs we’ve used as mitigations:
- cap max concurrent requests per TP replica
- disable CUDA graph capture for TP (if enabled)
- force extra stream sync path (perf hit but safer)

But we should be careful: some of these are only in certain versions / behind flags.

Sean Gallagher: +1 to version scoping. Runbook should have “Runtime version <1.19” vs “>=1.19 with watchdog” sections.

Aisha Bello: For alerts: we should add a runbook section “what alert fired / what panel to look at”:
- high `request_timeout_total` correlated to `pod`
- if/when we have it: `tp_heartbeat_gap_seconds` and `tp_stall_reason_total{reason=...}`

Tomáš Novák: Immediate follow-ups (today/tomorrow) we can action without waiting for new code:
- [ ] I’ll draft a runbook page with current commands + mitigation order, with big caveats on node access.
- [ ] Aisha: can you add a temporary dashboard note/panel that shows timeouts by pod for TP routes? Even if it’s janky.
- [ ] Rafael: can you add a short “when to drain” guideline for SREs (and ensure we have permissions documented)?

Rafael Mendes: Yep. Also adding explicit “collect logs before restart” note:
- last 10m runtime logs
- any NCCL debug snippet if enabled
- pod description/events

Jacob Stein: Add to commands:
```
kubectl -n runtime describe pod <pod>
# capture node name + events
```
And if we have the debug endpoint:
```
curl -s http://<pod-ip>:<port>/debug/runtime_state | head
```
(we should verify endpoint name; don’t want to publish fake commands.)

Omar El-Sayed: For runtime_state endpoint: it exists in some envs but not standardized. For now runbook should say “if `/_debug/runtime` is enabled in this cluster” and link to internal docs.

Sofia Ivanova: Also add guidance: don’t keep hammering a wedged replica with retries. If you see repeated timeouts from same pod, drain it fast. Retries pile up and make it worse.

Noah Patel: +1. Put this in bold. TP stalls tend to be sticky; letting load accumulate increases blast radius.

Sean Gallagher: Action item: once PR-21834/TP heartbeat lands, SRE needs to update the runbook to “preferred signals” and deprecate the guesswork.

Tomáš Novák: Noted. I’ll make the runbook draft explicitly call out “today: heuristics” and “post-1.19: use heartbeat + collective counters”.

Aisha Bello: Re: default thresholds, we can put placeholders:
- “If no tokens streamed for >60s (configurable by model), suspect stall”
- “If timeouts spike >X/min on same replica, drain”
We can refine later.

Rafael Mendes: Please also include the escalation checklist:
- page Runtime (Kernels/Scheduling) if >2 replicas wedge in 15m or if drain/restart doesn’t recover
- open INT helpdesk if we need temporary extra NCCL logging

Tomáš Novák: Got it. I’ll link to INT-2219 template once it’s created/updated.

Connor O'Brien: When you draft, include the exact Slack channels to coordinate: `#incidents`, `#eng-runtime`, `#eng-sre`, and `#eng-releases` if we’re changing flags.

Tomáš Novák: Will do. I’m going to start a Confluence page under eng-serving-runtime/runbooks and we can iterate. I’ll drop a link here once it’s up.

Sean Gallagher: Thanks. This is exactly the kind of “paper cut” that turns into hours during a Sev1. Let’s get it into the oncall rotation notes too.

Tomáš Novák: :thumbsup: I’ll post draft EOD.

source.body:
Miles Thornton: looking for quick feedback from folks who’ve used the v2 beta cards this week. are they too verbose? too sparse? what would you change for “first 5 min” context?

Aisha Bello: i like having it all, but the saturation card is a wall of text in Slack. i end up scrolling past it to get to the actual incident thread.

Jacob Stein: +1. the “config diff” is the worst offender. in one incident it dumped like 80 lines (even with redaction) and it drowned out the human updates.

Devon Price: yep Slack is also not super forgiving. Block Kit sections start getting gnarly once we’re > ~3k chars. we’re doing some truncation but it’s not opinionated enough.

Rafael Mendes: for oncall: default should be *summary only* with one-click expand. i want to know:
- did anything deploy in last ~60m for this service/region
- did config/routing change recently
- are we saturated (gpu/queue)
then links.

Mina Haddad: agreed. also the routing bits should highlight only the stuff that affects traffic decisions:
- fallback enabled/disabled
- model pin changes
- weight shifts / route policy changes
- quota / rate limit changes
not every field in the config tree.

Logan Wright: saturation card: prefer “top 3 signals” + whether they crossed threshold + trend arrow. raw numbers for everything is noisy.

Miles Thornton: can you be specific about “top 3”? which ones do you actually look at?

Logan Wright: in practice:
1) queue depth / admission control (if it’s growing, that’s the story)
2) gpu util (but only meaningful w/ queue)
3) kv cache pressure / evictions (if we’re churning)
then link to the saturation overview dashboard. everything else can be behind “details”.

Aisha Bello: + include error budget burn link per region. i keep hopping between 2-3 dashboards to confirm it’s not just a noisy p95.

Chloe Martin: deep link wise: please make sure the links land me in Console with the right filters already set. biggest pain today is “dashboard link” opens global view and i lose 60 seconds clicking around.

Jacob Stein: + the links should lock the time window to incident start-ish. like “last 30m” or “since thread created”. if it opens “last 24h” it’s not helpful.

Devon Price: we can do both:
- default time window = `thread_ts - 30m to now`
- include a secondary link “open last 24h” for context

Mina Haddad: another thing: truncation rules need to be deterministic. i saw a diff where the interesting change got cut off but a bunch of unchanged metadata made it in.

Devon Price: what’s the ideal truncation heuristic?

Mina Haddad: for config diff:
- show only changed paths (no surrounding context)
- cap at e.g. 10 changed paths
- if > 10, show “+N more changes” and force modal
- within a path, show only old/new scalar values, don’t dump full nested objects

Jacob Stein: + categorize changes. like:
“Routing policy: 2 changes”
“Feature flags: 1 change”
“Quotas: 3 changes”
then list the specific ones under each. easier to scan.

Aisha Bello: and give a redaction warning that’s not scary but clear. right now it says “REDACTED” a lot and ppl assume the bot is broken.

Miles Thornton: +1. people asked me if it was failing because half the diff says `[REDACTED]`.

Devon Price: we can adjust copy to: “Sensitive values hidden. Use break-glass to view full diff (audited).”

Rafael Mendes: please don’t push break-glass in the happy path. most incidents don’t need it. just make sure summary contains the non-sensitive part.

Chloe Martin: deep links i want per card:
- Deploys: link to deploy timeline (service + region + last 2h) + link to PR/commit
- Config diff: link to “Config changes” view for that tenant/env (but safe) + link to request elevated access
- Saturation: link to “Capacity & saturation” dashboard with pool/region preselected
- Errors: link to “SLO burn” + “Top 5 error routes”

Logan Wright: + for saturation: add a “copy query” action if possible. i sometimes want to run the exact PromQL/NRQL with tweaks.

Devon Price: Slack action buttons can do “Copy to clipboard” only in modals (not always), but we can show a code block with the query + “open in Grafana” link.

incident-bot: :information_source: FYI Slack message payload limit hit; long diffs will be moved to a modal automatically.

Jacob Stein: lol that’s what we want :thumbsup:

Miles Thornton: what about the “recent deploys” card — is it too sparse now?

Aisha Bello: deploy card is mostly good. only tweak: if there were no deploys, say “No deploys detected for service X in region Y in last 60m” (explicit) so i don’t wonder if it failed to query.

Rafael Mendes: + add canary status *only if* canary exists. otherwise it’s confusing. i saw “Canary: N/A” and wasted time thinking there was supposed to be one.

Devon Price: got it. we’ll hide canary row when not applicable.

Mina Haddad: also: if there *was* a deploy but it’s in staged rollout, show rollout phase clearly (ex: “Phase 1/3 - 10% EU-West only”). that’s actually the key triage clue.

Miles Thornton: cool. summarizing proposed changes:
- summary-first cards, details behind modal
- config diff truncation by changed paths, cap 10, group by category
- saturation: highlight queue/gpu/kv w/ thresholds + trend; details behind modal
- deep links must prefilter service/region/tenant and time window anchored to thread
- explicit “no data” vs “query failed” messaging

Dylan Brooks: +1 from tooling side. please also keep card height consistent. if saturation explodes the layout it pushes human updates below the fold.

Devon Price: i’ll cut a ticket for:
1) deterministic truncation + grouping
2) link parameterization w/ thread_ts window
3) “no data” vs “error” states

Miles Thornton: thanks all. keep feedback coming esp. with screenshots from real incidents.

Aisha Bello: will do. i’ll drop a snippet next time. :pray:

- model pin changes
- weight shifts / route policy changes
- quota / rate limit changes

Logan Wright: in practice:
1) queue depth / admission control (if it’s growing, that’s the story)

3) kv cache pressure / evictions (if we’re churning)

Jacob Stein: + the links should lock the time window to incident start-ish. like “last 30m” or “since thread created”. if it opens “last 24h” it’s not helpful.

Devon Price: we can do both:
- default time window = `thread_ts - 30m to now`
- include a secondary link “open last 24h” for context

Mina Haddad: for config diff:
- show only changed paths (no surrounding context)
- cap at e.g. 10 changed paths
- if > 10, show “+N more changes” and force modal
- within a path, show only old/new scalar values, don’t dump full nested objects

“Feature flags: 1 change”
“Quotas: 3 changes”

Miles Thornton: +1. people asked me if it was failing because half the diff says `[REDACTED]`.

Devon Price: we can adjust copy to: “Sensitive values hidden. Use break-glass to view full diff (audited).”

Rafael Mendes: please don’t push break-glass in the happy path. most incidents don’t need it. just make sure summary contains the non-sensitive part.

- Deploys: link to deploy timeline (service + region + last 2h) + link to PR/commit
- Config diff: link to “Config changes” view for that tenant/env (but safe) + link to request elevated access
- Saturation: link to “Capacity & saturation” dashboard with pool/region preselected
- Errors: link to “SLO burn” + “Top 5 error routes”

Logan Wright: + for saturation: add a “copy query” action if possible. i sometimes want to run the exact PromQL/NRQL with tweaks.

Devon Price: Slack action buttons can do “Copy to clipboard” only in modals (not always), but we can show a code block with the query + “open in Grafana” link.

incident-bot: :information_source: FYI Slack message payload limit hit; long diffs will be moved to a modal automatically.

Jacob Stein: lol that’s what we want :thumbsup:

Miles Thornton: what about the “recent deploys” card — is it too sparse now?

Aisha Bello: deploy card is mostly good. only tweak: if there were no deploys, say “No deploys detected for service X in region Y in last 60m” (explicit) so i don’t wonder if it failed to query.

Rafael Mendes: + add canary status *only if* canary exists. otherwise it’s confusing. i saw “Canary: N/A” and wasted time thinking there was supposed to be one.

Devon Price: got it. we’ll hide canary row when not applicable.

Mina Haddad: also: if there *was* a deploy but it’s in staged rollout, show rollout phase clearly (ex: “Phase 1/3 - 10% EU-West only”). that’s actually the key triage clue.

Miles Thornton: cool. summarizing proposed changes:
- summary-first cards, details behind modal
- config diff truncation by changed paths, cap 10, group by category
- saturation: highlight queue/gpu/kv w/ thresholds + trend; details behind modal
- deep links must prefilter service/region/tenant and time window anchored to thread
- explicit “no data” vs “query failed” messaging

Dylan Brooks: +1 from tooling side. please also keep card height consistent. if saturation explodes the layout it pushes human updates below the fold.

Devon Price: i’ll cut a ticket for:
1) deterministic truncation + grouping
2) link parameterization w/ thread_ts window
3) “no data” vs “error” states

Miles Thornton: thanks all. keep feedback coming esp. with screenshots from real incidents.

Aisha Bello: will do. i’ll drop a snippet next time. :pray:

source.body:
Dev Patel: quick q for whoever’s around — for the quant profile kill-switch, what *exactly* happens when we flip it? does it hard-disable profile application even if route config has `quantization_profile_ref` set?

Dev Patel: also in a rollback, what are the 2-3 things oncall should check to confirm we’re actually back to baseline?

Connor O'Brien: yep, kill-switch is a global override. when enabled, policy resolution returns “disabled” regardless of route-level `quantization_profile_ref` (even explicit profile IDs).

Connor O'Brien: mental model:
1) Route config says “default” / “none” / “profile:<id>”
2) Policy engine evaluates compatibility + tenant overrides
3) THEN global kill-switch is applied last and forces disable

Aisha Bello: +1. dashboards should show `quant_profile_applied_rate` drop to ~0 and `quant_profile_resolution_outcome{outcome="disabled"}` spike.

Benji Okafor: name of the flag in config is `quant_profiles.global_disable=true` (control plane reads it, cached ~30s; runtime sees it via resolved policy).

Dev Patel: ok, so even if customer keeps toggling in Console, it won’t take effect while kill-switch is on?

Ethan Park: correct. Console will still write the setting + audit it, but resolved policy will return disabled. (Console shows a banner “Global disable active” in prod once the backend returns the override bit.)

Chloe Martin: re Console behavior: user sees their route setting saved, but “Effective state” will show Disabled + reason = GLOBAL_KILL_SWITCH.

Mina Haddad: for rollback runbook, order we’ve been recommending:
1) disable profile at the impacted route(s) if it’s isolated (fast + minimal blast radius)
2) if broad / unknown scope, flip global kill-switch
3) follow up: remove/cleanup route refs once stable

Ravi Menon: also note: compatibility fallback vs kill-switch disable are distinct in logs/metrics. fallback happens when policy says “profile requested” but incompatible; kill-switch says “no profile allowed”.

Dev Patel: what do I check per-request to confirm baseline? is there a trace tag?

Noah Patel: runtime logs/traces include `quant_profile_id` + `quant_profile_resolution`.
- applied: `quant_profile_resolution=applied` and `quant_profile_id=<profile>`
- fallback: `quant_profile_resolution=fallback` (will often still include requested id + fallback_reason)
- disabled: `quant_profile_resolution=disabled` and `quant_profile_id=` (empty)

Noah Patel: in Honeycomb/Tracing you can filter span fields:
`quant_profile_resolution != "applied"` to verify we’re not applying.

Aisha Bello: quick checklist we’ve been using during rollback:
- Control plane: `GET /v1/routes/<route_id>/resolved-settings` should show `quantization_profile_effective=disabled` + reason
- Runtime: sample 20 requests => `quant_profile_resolution=disabled` (or absent field on older pods)
- Metrics: `fallback_rate` should go down; `p95_decode_ms` should revert within ~5-10 min; error rate normalizes

Benji Okafor: if you need to sanity check config propagation from a shell:
```bash
curl -sS -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  https://controlplane.prod.redwood.ai/v1/routes/$ROUTE_ID/resolved-settings \
| jq '.quantization'
```

Connor O'Brien: caveat: config caches. policy service cache TTL ~30s, edge routing cache ~60s. so expect 1-2 min for full convergence.

Connor O'Brien: if you’re in a P0 and need immediate effect, kill-switch + force config refresh rollout is the combo (but usually kill-switch is enough).

Incident Bot: (FYI) Runbook: rollback-procedure-quantization-profiles — https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/SRE/Rollback+Procedure+-+Quantization+Profiles

Dev Patel: nice. what about pods that already loaded the quant artifacts — do they keep using them after kill-switch?

Noah Patel: they may keep artifacts in memory, but they won’t be selected once resolved policy says disabled. selection is per-request; no “sticky” quant mode across requests.

Ravi Menon: we did have that fallback loop bug earlier (fixed) where policy kept re-evaluating; but kill-switch path bypasses that codepath. :thumbsup:

Aisha Bello: during rollback you can watch `quant_profile_metadata_fetch_qps` too — should drop once kill-switch is on (since we stop trying to resolve/apply profiles).

Chloe Martin: and audit trail: every kill-switch flip emits an audit event + shows in Console Admin -> Change History.

Dev Patel: perfect. last q: if customer complains “toggle missing” — could be because kill-switch is on?

Ethan Park: could be kill-switch OR RBAC/feature-flag mismatch. if kill-switch is on, toggle should still be visible but disabled with banner. if it’s missing entirely, check org feature flag + RBAC role.

Connor O'Brien: action item: I’ll add a 5-line oncall snippet to the runbook with the exact fields to search (`quant_profile_resolution`, `quant_profile_id`).

Dev Patel: thanks all, this is super clear. :pray:

source.text:
Miles Thornton: heads up for next week’s on-call rotations — we’re going to start seeing more nodes sit in `Validating` as provision-to-ready gates roll out. want to align on: what “Validating” *means*, when it’s “stuck”, when to quarantine, and who to page (Infra vs Platform).

Aisha Bello: +1. I’m updating the alert runbook and need crisp thresholds. right now folks see “Validating” and assume kube is broken.

Rafael Mendes: can we treat this thread as the on-call cheat sheet? I’ll paste into #eng-sre-runbooks after.

Vanessa Ortiz: yep. quick definition (per our state machine):
- `Provisioning`: instance boot + join steps (kubelet, runtime config, labels/taints)
- `Validating`: node has joined cluster, agent is running validations (gpu health, driver parity, net/storage probes, NCCL smoke, perf canary)
- `Ready`: validations passed, provisioning taint removed, node can be added to serving pool
- `Quarantined`: node failed repeatedly or hit a “hard fail” and is removed from rotation / cordoned

Jordan Lee: minor nuance: `Validating` is authoritative from Fleet Readiness Service (control plane), not “whatever kube says”. kube can be Ready while we’re still in Validating.

Miles Thornton: what should on-call interpret as “Validating but fine” vs “Validating stuck”?

Harish Reddy: practical ops view:
- Validating < ~10 min is normal for fresh nodes (esp NCCL + perf canary)
- if Validating > 25–30 min for a batch, something’s wrong (usually agent not running / NCCL hanging / network flake)

Aisha Bello: can we codify “stuck” threshold by step? the dashboard is step breakdown now.

Sergio Costa: dashboard has per-step durations. rule of thumb from staging:
- GPU health + driver parity: should be <2–3 min
- kube join checks: <5 min
- NCCL smoke: 3–8 min normally (longer if retries)
- perf canary: 2–5 min
if a single step is >15 min, that’s a smell.

Allison Grant: + reminder: look at step breakdown, not just overall Validating. overall can look scary but it’s usually one step timing out.

Miles Thornton: ok, decision tree for pager:
1) node stuck Validating — where do we look first?

Benji Okafor: first check Fleet Readiness Service view (not kube):
- internal endpoint: `/internal/fleet/readiness/nodes?pool_id=…` (or via console)
- it will show `current_state`, `last_transition_at`, and `failure_reason` if any.
If the service is returning stale state / 5xx, that’s Platform.

Ethan Park: +1. if you can’t fetch readiness state or transitions aren’t persisting, page Platform Control Plane.

Vanessa Ortiz: if readiness service is fine, check validation artifacts pointer (it stores where logs are). usually points at validation-agent logs or perf-canary run.

Rafael Mendes: what’s the “fast path” check if you’re mid-incident?

Harish Reddy: for a single node:
- `kubectl describe node <node>` — confirm taint `redwood.ai/provisioning=true:NoSchedule` still present
- `kubectl -n fleet-validation get pods -o wide | grep <node>` — validation agent running?
- if no agent pod scheduled → Infra (daemonset/helm/cluster config)
- if pod crashlooping → Infra (image/agent) unless error looks like authz to readiness API (then Platform)

Aisha Bello: can you paste example commands? people always ask.

Harish Reddy: sure:
`kubectl describe node ip-10-..` (look for taints + conditions)
`kubectl -n fleet-validation get pods -o wide --field-selector spec.nodeName=<node>`
`kubectl -n fleet-validation logs ds/node-validation-agent -c agent --since=30m | tail -200`
`kubectl -n perf-canary logs deploy/post-provision-canary --since=30m`

Sergio Costa: if it’s *many* nodes, start from Grafana:
- Provision-to-ready overview (time-to-ready + state counts)
- Step breakdown heatmap (step_name x region/gpu)
- Quarantine counts
Then drill into logs for the dominant failure.

Miles Thornton: quarantine criteria — when can on-call quarantine vs retry?

Vanessa Ortiz: policy we’re using in rollout:
- soft failures (timeouts, transient net checks): auto-retry up to 3 attempts. on-call can trigger “retry validation” once if it looks like a one-off.
- hard failures (GPU Xid, persistent ECC, driver mismatch, missing NVLink fabric, NCCL all-reduce consistently failing): quarantine immediately.

Rafael Mendes: can we define hard-fail signals more concretely?

Vanessa Ortiz: hard fail examples:
- `nvidia-smi` reports GPU fallen off bus
- Xid codes in dmesg repeated during validation
- ECC error rate above threshold (agent marks `gpu_health=fail`)
- driver/CUDA mismatch vs golden image channel (parity check fails)
- NCCL smoke test fails *twice* with same topology error (not a timeout)

Allison Grant: also: if validation failures spike right after an image channel change, don’t quarantine the world — consider rollback first. that’s Infra + Release Eng.

Connor O'Brien: +1. if you see a sharp step failure increase correlated w/ `image_channel=candidate` rollout, page Release Eng + Infra before mass quarantine. we can pull the kill switch.

Aisha Bello: paging guidance: Infra vs Platform vs SRE?

Miles Thornton: SRE is the *entry point* if an alert fires, but ownership is Infra/Platform depending on failure class. I’d like explicit “page X when Y” bullets.

Ethan Park: Platform page conditions:
- Fleet Readiness Service 5xx / latency
- state transitions not being recorded (events received but DB write failing)
- authz failures calling readiness mutation endpoints (403/401 in agent)
- mismatch between “validation says pass” but state never leaves Validating (control-plane logic / state machine)

Vanessa Ortiz: Infra page conditions:
- validation agent not scheduled / daemonset broken
- image bake regression (driver install, containerd/kubelet config)
- kube join failures (node never appears, or flaps NodeReady)
- NCCL failures that look like fabric/network config (not readiness API)
- provisioning orchestrator step failures / retries exhausted

Sergio Costa: “who owns perf canary?” it’s kinda infra-ish but runs app-ish code.

Vanessa Ortiz: perf canary failures that are “can’t pull image / can’t reach model endpoint / node networking” → Infra. if perf canary is failing because readiness service says “not allowed” or metadata missing (pool id, tokens) → Platform.

Rafael Mendes: quarantine vs page: if we have capacity shortfall risk, do we page immediately?

Harish Reddy: if quarantines are causing pool below committed capacity, page Infra + Capacity Ops immediately (Jamal usually). otherwise, quarantine is ok as long as replacement nodes are coming.

Aisha Bello: I’ll add note: “Quarantine reduces capacity; if pool utilization >80% and quarantine count rising, treat as customer-impacting risk.”

Incident Bot: Reminder: If customer impact is suspected, open an incident in #incidents and assign an IC/Comms. Template: https://incident-bot.redwood/runbooks/provisioning-failure

Miles Thornton: what about “Validating stuck but kube looks healthy” scenarios? folks get confused b/c NodeReady=true.

Jordan Lee: that’s expected. kube readiness != inference readiness. Validating is the gate. until the provisioning taint is removed + readiness state is Ready, the node should not join serving pools.

Benji Okafor: and if you see kube NodeReady=true but readiness state hasn’t moved in >30 min *and* no new validation artifacts are being written, that’s usually agent-heartbeat missing (Infra) or artifact ingestion failing (Platform). readiness service will show “last_agent_heartbeat_at” in the detail view — use that.

Aisha Bello: do we have a rule: heartbeat stale => Infra?

Ethan Park: mostly yes. if heartbeat missing because agent can’t auth to readiness (token expired / 401) that’s Platform-ish, but the symptom is in agent logs.

Vanessa Ortiz: practical: if `last_agent_heartbeat_at` >10m old → check agent pod logs first. if it’s crashlooping or not scheduled → Infra. if logs show `403` to readiness endpoint → Platform.

Rafael Mendes: ok, summarizing for on-call:
- “Validating” normal up to ~10–15m, investigate >25–30m
- Look at readiness service detail: failure_reason, current_step, last heartbeat
- Use step breakdown dashboard to find dominant stuck step
- Quarantine immediately on hard GPU health / driver parity / repeated NCCL topology errors
- Page Platform for readiness service issues / authz / state machine stuck with validations passed
- Page Infra for agent/daemonset, image, kube join, NCCL/fabric, orchestrator retries

Aisha Bello: :thumbsup: I’ll bake this into the on-call doc + link dashboards/runbook.

Connor O'Brien: can you also add “if correlated with image channel change, consider rollback / disable flag”?

Vanessa Ortiz: yep. and for next week rollout, we’re limiting to us-east A100; if on-call sees weirdness outside that scope, likely unrelated.

Miles Thornton: thanks all. if anyone has concrete error signatures (“NCCL WARN NET/Socket” etc) drop them here and I’ll add to runbook.

Harish Reddy: common ones from last week:
- `ncclSystemError: Socket recv failed` → usually flaky fabric; retry once, then Infra if repeat
- `CUDA driver version is insufficient` → image/driver mismatch → quarantine + Infra
- `readiness-api 403 forbidden: missing pool:write` → Platform authz/role mapping

Benji Okafor: + if you see `failed to persist validation artifact: timeout` in readiness svc logs, page Platform — that blocks transitions even when validations pass.

Aisha Bello: noted. I’ll post final cheat sheet in this channel once updated.

source.messages:
Rafael Mendes: on-call handoff for audit exporter incident from earlier (lag spike / downstream 429s). capturing what to watch + toggles. cc @Aisha Bello (taking primary)

Redwood Incident Bot: Incident INC-4471 status changed: MITIGATED → MONITORING

Rafael Mendes: TL;DR
- root cause: Splunk HEC rate-limiting (429) on 2 beta tenants → retries/backoff built up queue → export lag peaked ~38m
- mitigation: paused Splunk adapter for affected tenants + reduced batch size + verified no DLQ growth
- current: lag back < 5m; retry rate normalizing

Rafael Mendes: Dashboards to keep open tonight:
1) Grafana: Audit Exporter / Overview (lag + throughput)
   https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/audit-exporter-overview
   Panels to watch:
   - "p95 export lag (by destination)"
   - "events exported/sec (by tenant)"
   - "checkpoint age"
2) Grafana: Audit Exporter / Destinations
   https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/audit-exporter-destinations
   Panels:
   - "adapter HTTP status codes" (look for sustained 429/403)
   - "retry attempts" + "backoff seconds"
3) Queue/Storage dashboard (exporter internal)
   https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/audit-exporter-queues
   Panels:
   - "pending batches" (should not trend up)
   - "DLQ depth" (should be ~0; anything non-zero means investigate)

Aisha Bello: got it. what are the paging thresholds we’re using right now?

Rafael Mendes: from SRE draft (we used these in the incident):
- page if p95 export lag > 15m for 10m (any destination) OR > 30m for 5m (critical)
- page if delivery success rate < 99.5% over 30m (per adapter)
- ticket-only if 429s elevated but lag < 10m
(Sean can confirm if we’re tweaking, but this is what alert rules are set to as of today)

Sean Gallagher: confirming. alert rules match. note: we loosened 429 warning to ticket-only after the noise last week.

Rafael Mendes: Toggles / controls (use sparingly; prefer tenant-scoped first):
A) Pause exports per destination type (safe):
- control-plane admin: `POST /internal/audit-exports/{tenant_id}/pause` with body `{"destination":"splunk"}`
- UI (Console → Audit Exports → Actions → Pause) works if you have the SRE role
This stops new sends but keeps checkpoints; lag metric will stop increasing but backlog remains.

B) Global kill switch (last resort):
- feature flag: `siem_audit_export_beta_enabled` (tenant allowlist). DO NOT disable globally unless we’re melting down.
- if you must, flip via internal admin tool: https://admin.redwood.internal/flags/siem_audit_export_beta_enabled

C) Backpressure safety / auto-pause:
- exporter has `maxLagAutoPauseMinutes=60` (from PR-1119). If lag hits this, it auto-pauses the destination and emits an error.
- when it triggers, you’ll see alert "destination auto-paused" + log line `AUTO_PAUSE_MAX_LAG`.

Aisha Bello: if we pause, what’s the expected customer impact message?

Rafael Mendes: "exports temporarily paused due to downstream rate limiting; no audit events deleted; once resumed exporter will backfill from last checkpoint; duplicates possible (at-least-once)". don’t promise exact timestamps.

Logan Wright: adding: if customer asks "missing events", point them to dedup via `event_id` on their side. at-least-once semantics.

Rafael Mendes: Quick triage checklist if lag starts climbing again:
1) Identify adapter causing it:
   - check "p95 export lag by destination" + "HTTP status codes"
2) If 429s from Splunk:
   - confirm which tenant(s): panel "429s by tenant"
   - reduce pressure first (tenant pause Splunk) rather than global
3) If 403s from Datadog:
   - usually API key scope / wrong site (datadoghq.com vs datadoghq.eu). see SUP-18497 notes.
4) If S3 errors:
   - look for `AccessDenied` or KMS issues; often rotated role trust or KMS key policy.

Rafael Mendes: Commands / where to look:
- exporter logs (prod):
  `kubectl -n audit-exporter logs deploy/audit-exporter -c exporter --since=30m | rg "ERROR|429|AUTO_PAUSE|DLQ"`
- check tenant config state:
  `curl -sS -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" https://control-plane.redwood.internal/internal/audit-exports/{tenant_id}/status`
  (status shows last_success_ts, last_error, paused=true/false, checkpoint_ts)

Aisha Bello: what about DLQ handling? any gotchas?

Rafael Mendes: DLQ gotchas:
- DLQ depth should be ~0. if it starts growing, do NOT spam replay.
- replay tool (guardrailed) lives in exporter image:
  `audit-exporter replay --tenant <id> --destination splunk --from-dlq --max-batches 50`
- always check downstream health first; replay while Splunk still 429’ing just makes things worse.
- replay will create duplicates; tell customer to dedup by `event_id`.

Sean Gallagher: +1. if DLQ > 1000 batches or growing steadily, page secondary and consider pausing exports to prevent runaway.

Rafael Mendes: When to involve Security (Marcus/Kevin):
- any sign of export sending to an unrecognized host / destination tampering (possible exfil)
- if logs contain unexpected sensitive fields (PII) or raw request payloads (should be redacted)
- if we suspect credentials exposure (Splunk HEC token / Datadog key showing up in logs, even partially)
- if customer asks for compliance language re: retention vs export responsibilities beyond the FAQ

Marcus Lin: +1. also loop us in if you’re asked to widen egress allowlist for a non-standard SIEM endpoint. we need threat-model review.

Rafael Mendes: Notes from today’s incident specifics:
- affected tenants: 2 (beta) using Splunk HEC; both had customer-side limit set lower than expected
- we paused Splunk for tenant A for ~22m, then resumed after customer confirmed HEC throttle settings updated
- no evidence of dropped events in exporter; checkpoints monotonic; DLQ remained at 0
- watch for recurrence around top of hour (their SIEM pipelines seemed to burst)

Aisha Bello: thanks. who is the POC if I need customer comms?

Rafael Mendes: customer comms: route through TAM/CSM; for the two tenants today it was Liam O'Rourke + Miguel Santos. if urgent/outage, use #customer-success + incident channel.

Logan Wright: also FYI we deployed a small config change earlier: reduced default Splunk batch payload size (helps avoid 413/429 cascades). no rollback expected.

Sean Gallagher: alright. Aisha, page me if lag breaches 30m or if auto-pause triggers on more than 3 tenants.

Aisha Bello: ack. I’m on it. :saluting_face:

Rafael Mendes: handing off. I’ll stay available for ~30m if anything flares.

source.content:
Rafael Mendes: Following up on the playbook work — we keep losing time in escalations bc the support bundle doesn’t include *upgrade plan* + *last run logs*. Can we add an automated section for it? Ideally `redwood-private support-bundle --include=upgrade` just works.

Dev Patel: +1. Support needs a single artifact to ask for. Right now we tell customers “send installer logs” and they send 1 file from 3 weeks ago.

Allison Grant: Agree. From observability side, what we need for triage is consistent:
- upgrade plan artifact (what we *intended* to do)
- upgrade status/state (what we *did*)
- last run logs with enough context to see failure mode (precheck vs backup vs migration vs helm)
Also pls redact tokens/secrets.

Michael Nguyen: We can do this if the installer persists the plan + state in the same dir as the state machine (it does in the new flow). We just need support-bundle to pick them up.
Paths (default):
- `/var/lib/redwood-private/upgrade/state.json`
- `/var/lib/redwood-private/upgrade/plan.json`
- logs: `/var/log/redwood-private/upgrade/last-run.log` (we may need to standardize this; right now it’s `installer.log` + step logs)

Connor O'Brien: From Release Eng: support bundle should include the resolved version + artifact sources too (chart repo / registry / bundle manifest). Helps when it’s checksum mismatch vs bad release.

Owen Phillips: Also a common ask from customers: “prove rollback is supported”. If state includes `rollback_supported` and stage/step, include that JSON verbatim.

Kira Thompson: If we do this, I’ll update the on-call runbook to say “first step: generate support bundle with upgrade included”. But we need stable filenames so Support can find it without spelunking.

Devon Price: Who owns the support-bundle codepath? Is it in the installer repo or the main `redwood-private` CLI?

Michael Nguyen: It’s in the installer repo (shared lib) but invoked via `redwood-private` wrapper. We can add a new collector called `upgrade/` that just copies artifacts + runs a few `kubectl` commands.

Leila Farouk: For CI/e2e, I can add an assertion that support-bundle contains `upgrade/plan.json` + `upgrade/state.json` after a simulated failed upgrade. Just need final paths.

Rafael Mendes: Requirements from on-call:
1) Must include upgrade plan + last status from most recent attempt
2) Must include last run logs (at least installer + migration job logs if present)
3) Must be safe to request from customer (redaction; avoid dumping secrets)
4) Works for VPC + on-prem/airgapped

Allison Grant: Redaction: the big one is env vars / inline tokens in logs. Can we do a simple scrub pass (regex) before adding to tarball?
Examples to mask:
- `Authorization: Bearer ...`
- `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=...`
- `client_secret` / `oidc.clientSecret`
- kubeconfig user tokens

Michael Nguyen: Scrub is good but we should also avoid collecting high-risk sources. For upgrade section, we should *not* include:
- full `kubectl get secret -A -o yaml`
- helm values if it contains creds
We can include a *redacted* view of the redwood namespace configmap(s), or skip entirely.

Dev Patel: From Support perspective, minimum viable is fine. We mostly need to know what stage it died at + the errors.

Connor O'Brien: + include `compatibility_policy_result` if available. If upgrade path is blocked, that should be obvious in the bundle.

Devon Price: Proposed structure inside the tar.gz:
- `upgrade/summary.txt` (human readable)
- `upgrade/plan.json`
- `upgrade/state.json`
- `upgrade/last-run.log` (scrubbed)
- `upgrade/steps/` (optional, if present)
- `upgrade/k8s/` (safe commands only)
We can also include `upgrade/versions.txt` with chart/app/installer versions.

Owen Phillips: For k8s logs: can we include these *scoped* to `redwood` namespace?
- `kubectl get pods -n redwood -o wide`
- `kubectl describe job -n redwood redwood-db-migrate` (if it exists)
- `kubectl logs -n redwood job/redwood-db-migrate --tail=2000` (cap size)
This avoids a lot of noise and risk.

Allison Grant: Size cap is important. Some customers have insane log volume. Add “max bytes” per file + “tail N”.

Michael Nguyen: We can do:
- always tail last 5k lines (or last 10MB) per log
- prefer the most recent run id directory
We already write run IDs in state: `run_id=2025-05-xxT...`.

Kira Thompson: What about when state dir doesn’t exist (old versions)?

Michael Nguyen: Then support bundle should degrade gracefully:
- include `upgrade/summary.txt` that says “no upgrade state found”
- include whatever legacy installer log exists (`/var/log/redwood-private/installer.log`) if present
But no hard failure.

support-bot: Reminder: when requesting logs from customers, include the “Redaction Notice” template in the reply macro. (Macro: `PRIVATE_SUPPORT_BUNDLE_REQUEST`)

Dev Patel: ↑ nice. Once we have stable filenames, I’ll update the macro to: 
“Run: `redwood-private support-bundle --include=upgrade`” and attach the tarball.

Rafael Mendes: Owner/next steps?

Devon Price: I can open a PR to add the collector + file layout this week. Need signoff on exact filenames.

Allison Grant: Filenames look good. Can we make `upgrade/summary.txt` include:
- target version
- current version
- last stage + step
- rollback_supported true/false
- precheck pass/fail counts
So on-call can eyeball quickly.

Connor O'Brien: Also include artifact source info (registry/chart repo/mirror endpoint) in summary. This is where half the airgapped issues are.

Leila Farouk: If Devon lands the collector, I’ll add e2e coverage next sprint. Just tag me on the PR.

Michael Nguyen: I’ll double check state/plan paths and ensure we always persist them before executing any mutating step (so even precheck failures have artifacts).

Kira Thompson: Once PR merges, I’ll add a short runbook note: “Before rollback, generate support bundle and attach upgrade section.”

Rafael Mendes: Cool. Action items:
- Devon: PR for support-bundle `upgrade/` collector + redaction + caps
- Michael: confirm canonical paths + ensure plan/state always written
- Allison/Connor: review summary fields + redaction patterns
- Leila: add test assertion
- Dev: update Support macro once we have the command + sample output

Devon Price: :thumbsup: I’ll post the PR link here when up.

Rafael Mendes: We need to update the on-call playbook for Private upgrade failures. Biggest gap right now: when do we tell customers “rollback now” vs “manual fix and continue”. Can folks weigh in on decision points + timeboxes?

Sean Gallagher: +1. We keep debating in the moment and it burns time. We should codify: (a) failure before any state change, (b) failure during CP rollout, (c) failure during DB migration, (d) failure after DP starts.

Dev Patel: From Support POV, we need something we can say confidently. Right now we oscillate between “try X” and “roll back” and it reads like uncertainty. If we can point to a doc section + a couple questions to ask (backup taken? migration started? etc.), that’d help.

Owen Phillips: Real example from last week: upgrade precheck passed, CP upgraded, then auth/OIDC reachability changed due to customer’s IdP maintenance. Everything was “down” but rollback wouldn’t have fixed it. We need a rule like “if failure is environmental (DNS/OIDC/registry access) and no irreversible changes, prefer manual fix.”

Kira Thompson: I’m editing the runbook doc anyway. Can we agree on a short decision tree at the top of the “Upgrade failures” section?

Connor O'Brien: Please include Release Eng guidance too: if it’s a known-bad version, rollback is recommended; if it’s artifact fetch or checksum mismatch, don’t rollback, just correct artifact source and re-run.

Michael Nguyen: DB migrations are the scary part. We should explicitly say: if migration step has started and we see lock timeouts / long-running transactions, don’t “retry randomly”. Either (1) wait within timebox, or (2) rollback only if migration is marked backward-compatible + installer indicates safe rollback.

Allison Grant: Also add “capture evidence” before rollback: grab support bundle + last upgrade state. We’re missing upgrade plan + logs in a bunch of escalations.

Vanessa Ortiz: For fleet ops: recommend rollback if DP is partially rolled and we’re seeing crashlooping across inference pods with no quick fix. Manual fix if it’s a single node issue or capacity shortage.

Rafael Mendes: Let’s propose concrete rules. Here’s a strawman:
1) If failure happens during *prechecks* (storage headroom, RBAC, OIDC, DNS, artifact availability): do NOT rollback (nothing changed). Fix environment and re-run.
2) If failure happens during *backup step*: do NOT proceed. If backup incomplete, do NOT continue upgrade. No rollback needed, but mark upgrade as aborted.
3) If failure happens during *control plane upgrade* and CP pods never become Ready within 15 min: timebox manual triage 15 min; if still failing and previous version is available + rollback hooks say safe, recommend rollback.
4) If failure happens during *DB migration*: follow migration playbook; timebox 30 min. Rollback only if installer state says migration is rollback-safe; otherwise require eng escalation.
5) If failure happens during *data plane rollout* and error rates breach SLO for >10 min: prefer rollback (restore previous DP chart release) unless root cause is external dependency.

Sean Gallagher: Mostly agree. Two tweaks:
- CP readiness timebox depends on cluster size. Maybe: “15 min for typical (<50 nodes), 30 min for large.” Keep it simple though.
- Add “hard stop” conditions: if we see corruption risk or unknown migration status, do NOT rollback without eng.

Michael Nguyen: Strongly want explicit “do not rollback” for these cases:
- migration status unknown (installer state missing / db migration job logs unavailable)
- migration applied with non-backward-compatible changes (rare, but we should gate)
- customer manually edited schema / custom extensions

Connor O'Brien: We can lean on installer state machine here. In the new flow, the upgrade writes state like `stage=db_migration`, `step=apply`, `rollback_supported=true/false`. The playbook should say “check state first, don’t guess.”

Kira Thompson: Can you paste the exact commands you want on-call/support to run? I’ll put them in the doc.

Rafael Mendes: Proposed minimal commands:
- Get status:
  `redwood-private upgrade status --output=summary`
- Fetch last state (for escalation):
  `redwood-private upgrade status --output=json > upgrade-status.json`
- Pull support bundle (includes upgrade plan + logs):
  `redwood-private support-bundle --include=upgrade`
- Rollback (only if status indicates rollback_supported):
  `redwood-private upgrade rollback --reason "cp_not_ready"`

Dev Patel: We also need “what questions to ask customer” section:
- Are you in a change window? When does it end?
- Are external deps stable (IdP, DNS, artifact registry, KMS)?
- Was backup step completed successfully? (screenshot / log line)
- Are they VPC or on-prem airgapped? (affects artifact access)

Owen Phillips: + add “airgapped gotchas”: checksum/signature errors are not rollback triggers. Usually it’s: wrong bundle path, stale manifest, or proxy rewriting downloads.

Allison Grant: For observability, can we standardize “annotate upgrade window” in the playbook too? Otherwise dashboards are noisy. Something like: “post in #incidents or use /ops annotate” (whatever we have).

Sean Gallagher: incident-bot can do it. If we’re in a customer incident, we should open one anyway. For internal on-call handling, maybe simpler: “drop a note in #incidents with env + version”.

incident-bot: Reminder: use `/incident create` for customer-impacting events. Add tag `private-upgrade` for routing.

Vanessa Ortiz: Also include a “manual fix first” list that’s safe:
- fix DNS / TLS secret, rerun helm hook job
- increase storage / expand PVC, rerun precheck
- restore registry access / correct artifact mirror endpoint
- cordon + drain a bad node if it’s just one
But warn: do not delete migration jobs/pods without guidance.

Michael Nguyen: Yes, and specifically “do NOT”:
- `kubectl delete job db-migrate` repeatedly
- manually rerun migration container outside installer (breaks state machine)
- edit `redwood-config` configmap mid-upgrade

Connor O'Brien: For playbook, we should connect to release gating: “if upgrade failure matches known issue in release notes, rollback is default.” We’ll keep a list per train.

Kira Thompson: Ok. I’ll draft the decision tree + command snippets + do/don’t list. Owners?

Rafael Mendes: Action items:
- Kira: draft playbook section + decision tree (today/tomorrow)
- Michael: provide migration-specific rollback-safe conditions text + timeboxes
- Connor: provide release-train note template + “known-bad => rollback” language
- Allison: add “evidence capture” checklist + where logs go
- Dev/Owen: add support-facing questions + customer messaging

Sean Gallagher: Add one more: “Escalate to eng” criteria. Suggest:
- rollback_supported=false but outage ongoing
- migration running > 30 min with lock contention
- repeated CP crashloop after reverting config

Dev Patel: Works. Also would like a canned line: “Rollback restores service faster but may revert new features; manual fix is preferable when failure is external/environmental.”

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: Let’s get this into the on-call handbook before the beta. Please reply here when your bits are ready so Kira can merge.

source.thread:
Rafael Mendes: seeing a bunch of noise from the new Dedicated throughput SLO burn-rate alerts in the last 24h. before we roll this wider, can we do a quick pass on what’s legit vs instrumentation? I’m mostly seeing `dedicated_throughput_slo_burnrate_6h` paging but dashboards don’t show customer impact.

Redwood Oncall Bot: :rotating_light: Paging alert triggered: `dedicated_throughput_slo_burnrate_6h` (service=dedicated-inference, region=us-east, deployment_id=ded-7f2c) - burn_rate=3.7, window=6h, slo=throughput_floor

Aisha Bello: yeah I got paged twice overnight. both times the “SLO status” panel dipped to ~92-94% for a few minutes, then recovered. looked like bursts + cold pods.

Sean Gallagher: can we confirm which evaluator we’re using in prod right now? is it the sliding window from slo-toolkit PR-311 or the older query-only burn-rate rule?

Aisha Bello: rules are coming from the config in redwood PR-19405. evaluator is slo-toolkit, but the alert is still a direct promql burn-rate calc (not the offline summary).

Sergio Costa: I pulled an example. around the page time, saturation looked fine but `tokens_out_total` had a spike + then a drop. also some deployments have missing `model_version` label so the group_by is weird.

Logan Wright: missing labels would cause series churn + bad rate calcs. do we know if this correlates with the metrics pipeline change from ENG-19244 rollout?

Aisha Bello: probably. also seeing a lot of 0s in `dedicated:throughput_tokens_per_sec` when the deployment is idle. the burn-rate math doesn’t like idle periods.

Sean Gallagher: if the SLO is defined as “meet throughput floor when offered load >= floor”, we should be excluding idle / under-offered-load minutes from the denominator.

Rafael Mendes: +1. right now it’s effectively treating “no traffic” as “0 throughput”, which burns error budget even though customer isn’t asking for anything.

Allison Grant: that’s consistent with the metric semantics doc draft, but we didn’t enforce it in the alert query. we need an “eligible minutes” filter (offered_load >= committed_floor * some epsilon).

Mina Haddad: also some of these pages coincide with admission control shedding. if we’re shedding because customer is above committed + burst, the SLO shouldn’t count it as a miss (depending on tier). are we tagging “rejected_due_to_overcommit” separately?

Keiko Yamamoto: we do have `admission_decision` labels in the new guardrail metrics, but the throughput series is only looking at tokens/sec. we could use `requests_admitted_total` vs `requests_rejected_total` to infer “offered load”.

Owen Phillips: I dug into SUP-18510 (streaming mismatch). there was also a double-counting bug for streaming token accounting. if the numerator is wrong (or negative after correction) we get spikes/dips.

Aisha Bello: yep, PR-19422 was merged yesterday to fix double-counting streaming. but in the window around the pages, we still had a mix of old/new pods so the rate graph looks jagged.

Rafael Mendes: ok so likely 3 contributors: (1) idle minutes counted as misses, (2) label churn / missing labels, (3) streaming accounting rollout causing transient weird rates.

Sean Gallagher: for (1), proposed change: gate SLO evaluation on `offered_tokens_per_sec >= 0.9 * committed_floor`. we can approximate offered via `tokens_requested` (prompt+max_tokens) rather than emitted tokens.

Logan Wright: we do emit `tokens_requested_total` per deployment now (post ENG-19244), but it’s not on every code path. I can patch the missing instrumentation in the legacy streaming handler.

Sergio Costa: for dashboards, can add a panel showing “eligible minutes %” so oncall can see if burn is real. right now people just see throughput dip with no context.

Allison Grant: for (2), missing `model_version` label: if it’s absent we should set it to `unknown` at the exporter (so grouping is stable). query-side coalesce is messy.

Aisha Bello: +1. alert rule currently groups by `deployment_id, region, model, model_version`. missing label = new series set = rate resets.

Mina Haddad: for (3), can we suppress pages during progressive rollout windows? feature flag deploys are giving us mixed accounting.

Rafael Mendes: we can add a 30m “deploy suppression” based on `build_info` change or `rollout_in_progress` label. but I’d rather fix the query so it’s resilient.

Sean Gallagher: agree. for burn-rate, we should also adjust thresholds. current is:
- page if 6h burn_rate > 2 AND 30m burn_rate > 14 (classic multiwindow)
that’s super sensitive if the denominator is fluctuating.

Aisha Bello: the pages I saw were almost all 30m burn_rate spikes. if we keep the multiwindow, maybe raise the short window threshold or widen to 1h/6h.

Rafael Mendes: action proposal (so we can ship before next week’s broader rollout):
1) update alert query to only count eligible minutes where offered load >= floor (Aisha + Sean)
2) patch exporter to always set model_version (Logan)
3) add dashboard panel: eligible minutes + offered load vs committed floor (Sergio)
4) temporarily downgrade the alert to ticket (no page) until (1) is in prod

Aisha Bello: works for me. I can open a PR against the alert config today. need a concrete promql definition for eligible.

Sean Gallagher: here’s a strawman:
- offered = rate(tokens_requested_total[5m])
- achieved = rate(tokens_emitted_total[5m])
- eligible = offered >= (0.9 * committed_floor_tokens_per_sec)
- sli = achieved / committed_floor, but only over eligible samples
We can implement as: `sum_over_time((achieved >= floor) and eligible)[window] / sum_over_time(eligible)[window]`

Allison Grant: careful: `and` in promql can drop labels unexpectedly. might need `* on(deployment_id) group_left` joins.

Aisha Bello: yeah. I’ll prototype in a scratch dashboard and paste the query.

Owen Phillips: also, for customers that set `max_tokens` huge but cancel early, requested tokens overestimates offered load. not sure if that matters, but could cause eligible=true when they’re not actually demanding that throughput.

Sean Gallagher: fair point. we can use `tokens_in_total + tokens_out_total` as offered approximation instead of requested. downside is it’s closer to achieved.

Mina Haddad: what about queue depth? if queue depth > 0 and admitted requests > 0, that’s clearly offered load beyond capacity.

Keiko Yamamoto: we have `dedicated_queue_depth` per deployment from the admission control PR. might be the cleanest eligibility signal: eligible if `queue_depth_p95 > 0` OR `admitted_rps > 0` above some threshold.

Logan Wright: queue depth metric is newer; coverage may not be 100% yet.

Rafael Mendes: let’s do a practical first pass: eligibility based on `rate(tokens_out_total + tokens_in_total)` (actual processed tokens) >= 0.5*floor. then tighten once requested tokens is fully reliable.

Sean Gallagher: ok, I’ll sign off on that for preview phase. we should document that eligibility is based on processed load for now.

Aisha Bello: cool. I’ll:
- change short window to 1h (vs 30m)
- add eligibility filter using processed tokens
- downgrade paging to ticket for 48h while we validate

Sergio Costa: I’ll add a “why are we paging” row: burn-rate, eligible minutes, and a link to drilldown per deployment. should help oncall.

Allison Grant: please also add an annotation for deploy events on the dashboard so people can correlate those spikes.

Redwood Oncall Bot: :information_source: Alert status changed: `dedicated_throughput_slo_burnrate_6h` acknowledged by Rafael Mendes

Rafael Mendes: thanks all. can we target having the tuned rules in staging today, prod tomorrow morning? I’ll update oncall handoff notes.

Aisha Bello: yup. will post PR link once up.

Sean Gallagher: I’ll update the SLO semantics doc to reflect eligibility (preview) + note streaming accounting rollout caveat.

Owen Phillips: I’ll comment on SUP-18510 with the conclusion (alert noise + streaming mixed version) so CS has context.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: let’s regroup tomorrow after we see if pages drop.

source.content:
Miles Thornton: trying to capture “first 5 min” context we always ask for in #incidents. if we can make incident-bot v2 spit this out automatically we save so much time. what’s MUST HAVE vs nice-to-have?

Jacob Stein: +1. i want a crisp checklist. maybe 3 cards: deploys, config diffs, saturation/health.

Aisha Bello: agree. and it needs to map to SLO burn patterns (latency vs 5xx vs streaming disconnects) so we’re not dumping noise.

Dylan Brooks: constraints reminder: bot has to be useful even if telemetry is slow/unhealthy. so it should degrade to cached data + deep links. keep queries bounded.

Devon Price: also Slack UX: one message with cards, not 20 lines. action buttons to expand details or open dashboards.

Miles Thornton: ok let’s enumerate. here’s my strawman for required fields:
- incident metadata: service, env, region(s), tenant/customer (if known), time window
- deploy context: last 3 deploys + rollout phase + canary status
- config context: “what changed?” in last ~60m (flags/routing/quota/model pins)
- saturation: GPU util, queue depth, KV cache pressure, backpressure/admission control
- links: traces/logs/dashboards, error budget burn

Connor O'Brien: deploy card needs: service -> image SHA + deploy start time + current rollout step. and link to the PR/commit.

Sahana Iyer: also need “rollout controller thinks it’s complete” vs “still propagating.” half our incidents are partial propagation.

Priya Natarajan: from platform side: config is tricky bc tenant safe. but we can do a diff for a safe subset.

Benji Okafor: config diff should be *scoped*: global defaults vs per-tenant overrides vs per-region. otherwise you get confusing diffs.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: +1 on tenant safety. we need redaction + access control. bot should never paste secrets into Slack. we should assume channels aren’t restricted.

Jacob Stein: must-have config fields for oncall:
- feature flags (route-level, safety switches)
- routing policy changes (fallback enablement, region weights)
- rate limit / quota changes
- model pin / model version changes
- any “circuit breaker” toggles

Mina Haddad: routing context specifically:
- primary model/variant, fallback chain
- region routing weights / spillover settings
- “graceful degradation mode” toggles (e.g., disable streaming, reduce max_tokens)
- last config push id + who/what system did it

Ravi Menon: + note whether fallback is currently happening (and at what rate). useful for knowing if we should intentionally widen fallback vs it’s already in motion.

Jordan Lee: we should include “policy evaluation summary” for a sample request: why it routed to X. but maybe that’s a button, not default.

Logan Wright: saturation signals: GPU util alone is noisy. for first 5 min i’d rather see:
- queue depth / time in queue (p50/p95)
- scheduler backpressure / admission rejects
- KV cache hit rate / evictions / pressure
- batch size distribution shifts (if available)
GPU util as supporting, not the headline.

Allison Grant: for alerting alignment, the bot should show which SLO is burning + error budget burn rate link. then recommended signals: e.g.
- 5xx burn -> deploy + config + admission/backpressure
- latency burn -> queue depth + kv cache + batching
- streaming disconnect -> deploy + proxy timeouts + regional saturation

Devon Price: from UX: we can have a top “Summary” row:
- SLO burning? yes/no + burn rate
- likely class: deploy / config / capacity / unknown
then three expandable sections.

Miles Thornton: gaps today (pain):
1) deploy context: we ask “did anything deploy?” and people paste a bunch of deploybot logs manually
2) config changes: we grep config service / look at console history (if you remember where)
3) saturation: everyone opens different dashboards, and metrics labels differ between hosted vs dedicated

Connor O'Brien: deploy gap is real bc it’s not joined by service consistently. we have deploy events but mapping to service/region is messy.

Sahana Iyer: + canary results aren’t in one place. sometimes canary failed but rollout continued bc threshold config.

Priya Natarajan: config gap: audit trail exists but “what changed” is hard. we need a diff API with allowlist + redaction.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: and we need audit events whenever bot accesses config diffs. esp if it’s tenant scoped.

Jacob Stein: saturation gap: KV cache pressure isn’t a standard metric today across pools. some teams use `kv_cache_evictions_total`, others `cache_pressure_ratio`.

Logan Wright: yep. standardization needed. also query cost/cardinality: bot cannot run 10 heavy queries per click in an incident.

Allison Grant: suggestion: bot uses a single “telemetry query service” that does pre-aggregated queries with strict time windows (last 15m/60m) and fixed label sets.

Dylan Brooks: + caching + request coalescing per thread. bot should say “cached 2m ago” so people trust it.

Mina Haddad: also important for first 5 min: scope. i always ask: is it one region? one tenant? one route/model? bot should show split if possible.

Ravi Menon: + if degradation levers exist, bot should surface the ones that are safe to pull:
- widen fallback
- reduce max_tokens
- disable streaming
- clamp concurrency
but maybe those are “suggested actions” behind a button.

Devon Price: careful: buttons that *change* config might be too risky. but we can show the current levers + link to runbook. interactive actions can be “open diff”, “open dashboard”, “copy command”.

incident-bot: (system) FYI: Current incident context payload includes: service name (best-effort), region (if parsed), and last 5 error log lines. No deploy/config/saturation context is attached.

Miles Thornton: lol thanks bot.

Jacob Stein: can we nail a minimal required list?

Aisha Bello: my “required” for first message:
- service + region + time window
- SLO burn link (or at least top error/latency chart)
- recent deploys (last 3) w/ rollout state
- config diff summary (safe) in last 60m
- saturation summary (queue + backpressure + gpu + kv cache)
- deep links to dashboards + traces/logs

Connor O'Brien: deploy detail fields:
- deploy_id
- image SHA
- commit SHA
- rollout_phase (canary/50%/100%)
- canary health (pass/fail + metric)
- link: PR + deploy dashboard

Benji Okafor: config diff summary should be something like:
“changed keys (allowlisted): routing.policy.weights, feature_flags.streaming_enabled, quotas.tokens_per_minute”
with values truncated + redacted where needed.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: + show “redacted N fields” so it’s obvious. and if someone needs full diff, they can request elevated access / break-glass via proper channel.

Logan Wright: saturation summary format idea:
- queue depth p95: X (threshold Y)
- admission rejects: X/min
- KV evictions: X/s
- GPU util: X%
and highlight the ones breaching.

Allison Grant: please include a consistent threshold reference. responders need “is this bad?” instantly.

Mina Haddad: routing summary: “traffic distribution” across regions/models (even coarse) + “fallback active?” yes/no.

Jordan Lee: + config version/hash currently applied. we debug “did my policy change actually take effect?” all the time.

Devon Price: UX note: we can keep it short by default:
- 3 bullets per card max
- “Show more” opens a modal with full details

Dylan Brooks: ok, action items i’m hearing:
1) define payload schema + required fields
2) define data sources + auth/audit requirements
3) define standardized saturation metrics + thresholds
4) define dashboard deep link templates

Miles Thornton: yep. also: what’s the time window? i’d suggest last 60m for deploy/config, last 15m for saturation.

Aisha Bello: + for SLO burn, last 30m default with quick link to last 6h.

Connor O'Brien: last 60m deploys is fine, but include “older deploy still rolling out” even if started >60m ago.

Sahana Iyer: good catch. rollouts can take 2h for some services.

Priya Natarajan: for config: include last successful push + last failed push.

Benji Okafor: and “partial propagation” detection would be amazing (different config versions across regions).

Logan Wright: for saturation: must support hosted + dedicated pools. labels differ. we need a normalized layer.

Allison Grant: also keep bot from DDoS’ing telemetry during incidents. hard limits + circuit breaker.

Devon Price: can we capture “missing context” states explicitly? e.g. “deploy context unavailable (service mapping missing)” with a link to manual command.

Jacob Stein: yes. the worst is empty cards with no explanation.

Miles Thornton: summarizing the consensus for doc:
MUST HAVE (first 5 min):
- Service/region/tenant + timeframe
- Recent deploys w/ rollout & canary status + PR/commit links
- Customer-safe config diff summary (flags/routing/quota/model pins) + redaction indicator
- Saturation summary (queue/backpressure/KV cache/GPU) w/ thresholds
- SLO burn + deep links (dashboards, traces, logs)
CURRENT GAPS:
- deploy metadata not joined reliably to service/region
- no config diff API w/ allowlist/redaction/audit
- saturation metrics not standardized; query cost too high; dashboards inconsistent

Miles Thornton: i’ll paste this into the kickoff doc + tag @Rafael Mendes and @Paula Mendes. keep adding if we missed something.

Ravi Menon: add one more must-have: “graceful degradation config currently set to …” (even if read-only). we keep guessing.

Mina Haddad: +1. read-only is fine. show current fallback chain + whether spillover is enabled.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: last note: bot output in #eng-oncall is ok, but in #incidents we should assume broader audience. default to safe output always.

Dylan Brooks: agreed. ok i’ll spin a Linear ticket for payload schema + UX cards based on this thread.

Devon Price: :thumbsup: once you have schema i can mock the Slack blocks + modal pagination.

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: i’ll draft the thresholds for saturation and tie to alert tuning.

Logan Wright: i’ll audit which metrics we can standardize quickly vs need pipeline work.

Connor O'Brien: i’ll follow up on deploy event join + canary status source.

Miles Thornton: thanks all. this is exactly what we need.

Peter Holtz: Heads up for anyone on oncall this week — we need a crisp playbook for “rollout gate failed” on Dedicated staged rollouts (beta). What do we do, who do we page, what do we capture?

Rafael Mendes: +1. Last time we wasted ~30m figuring out if it was real vs telemetry lag. Let’s write down decision points.

Sean Gallagher: Can we align on defaults?
- If gate fail indicates customer impact (429s, p99 blowup, error rate), abort immediately.
- If gate fail is missing data/unknown, hold stage and investigate telemetry.
- Force-promote only with explicit approval + audit reason.

Mina Haddad: From routing side: if we abort, traffic split reverts to previous stable partition + region fallback rules kick in. But abort needs to happen fast or we keep starving capacity in canary partition.

Logan Wright: Telemetry note: gate evaluator is fail-closed on “stale/missing” by design (per Priya’s email). So “gate failed: data_missing” is expected during ingest lag.

Vanessa Ortiz: And on small pools, the 1% stage can still be “1 replica” which is effectively 10–20% depending on pool size. So if we see saturation/queue depth spike at canary, abort is usually correct.

Peter Holtz: Ok, let’s draft a checklist here. Please poke holes.

Peter Holtz: Proposed oncall steps when gate fails (Dedicated staged rollouts):
1) Confirm scope + severity
   - Identify: `account_id`, `pool_id`, `rollout_plan_id`, `current_stage`, `target_stage`, `region(s)`.
   - Check if customer facing symptoms exist: 5xx, 429, elevated latency, dropped streams.
   - If customer impact confirmed OR error budget burn high => go to (3) ABORT.
2) Classify gate failure type
   A) “Signal failure” (telemetry missing/stale): latency/error panels blank, evaluator says missing data.
   B) “Real regression” (metrics breached): error rate/p99/saturation/oom gate breach.
   C) “Capacity partitioning artifact” (canary too small): queue depth up + 429s but overall fleet healthy.
3) Execute action
   - ABORT if any of:
     * 429 gate failed (sustained) OR drop rate gate failed
     * p99 latency gate failed + saturation > threshold
     * OOM rate gate failed
     * GPU health gate failed (node taints/ECC spike) with customer impact
   - HOLD if:
     * gates show `unknown`/missing data and no customer impact
     * region impairment suspected and multi-region pool is partially degraded
   - FORCE-PROMOTE only if:
     * SRE + owner approval AND gates failing due to known false positive (e.g., telemetry lag) AND customer wants to continue
     * must include reason + link to incident/ticket
4) Page/escalate
   - Page SRE oncall if customer impact, or if abort doesn’t stabilize within 10m.
   - Page Routing oncall (@Mina / @Ravi’s rotation) if symptoms are 429/queue depth/capacity starvation.
   - Page Telemetry oncall (@Logan) if “missing data” and dashboards are blank / staleness alerts.
   - Page Fleet/Infra (@Vanessa/@Irene) if GPU health gate or region capacity constraints.
5) Capture evidence (for audit + postmortem)
   - Gate evaluator output (copy/paste) including observed value, threshold, window, sample size.
   - Links to dashboards (stage view) + screenshots if possible.
   - Audit event IDs for promote/abort/hold + actor + reason.
   - Feature flag state for staged rollouts + pool partition config.
   - Any notable routing logs / trace IDs / request IDs.

Peter Holtz: Missing pieces? (esp thresholds / who to page)

Allison Grant: For evidence capture: also note “metrics freshness” (ingest lag) if you’re classifying as signal failure.
We have the staleness panel on the stage dashboard wireframe. In prod it’s `telemetry_ingest_lag_seconds`.

Jordan Lee: + add “idempotency key” if someone retries an abort/promote. We’ve seen double-clicks. The action API response includes `action_id` + `idempotency_key` in headers.

Chloe Martin: Console side: if gate fails, Console shows “Gate details” drawer. Please copy the “Gate evaluation JSON” block from the drawer (it includes query + resolved window). That’s the thing we need for debugging.

Connor O'Brien: Release-eng request: if abort was triggered during a deploy window, note the control-plane version / runtime build SHA and whether any migrations were mid-flight. That helps us determine rollback safety.

Mina Haddad: For escalation matrix: I’d add a fast path:
- If 429 gate fails at canary stage: abort first, page routing second.
We can debug after capacity is restored.

Vanessa Ortiz: Agree. Also, if pool is < 8 GPUs, don’t try to “wait it out” in canary. Partition granularity is too chunky. Abort, then revisit stage definition.

Rafael Mendes: Let’s be explicit about “stabilize within 10m” definition:
- error rate back under gate threshold for 2 consecutive windows
- 429 rate normalized + queue depth trending down
- p99 back to within +X% baseline
If not, keep incident open.

Sean Gallagher: Paging: who is “SRE oncall” for this? We should reference rotations.
Maybe:
- `#eng-sre` for page + `@sre-oncall`
- `#eng-platform` for policy engine/control plane
- `#eng-infra` for fleet
- `#incidents` if customer impact (open incident)

Peter Holtz: Good call. We can use group mentions rather than names.

Logan Wright: For telemetry lag classification: gate evaluator includes `data_freshness` field (max age of samples). If it’s > 180s it’s probably ingest lag; if it’s “no series found” it’s likely tag mismatch (stage_id label missing) — different fix.

Allison Grant: +1 on tag mismatch. We had that Console issue where stage evidence drawer was empty b/c stage tag was `stage` vs `stage_id`. If you see “no series found” for all gates, suspect tagging.

Jordan Lee: Another important capture: rollout state machine status.
- `RUNNING`, `HELD`, `ABORTING`, `ABORTED`, `COMPLETED`.
Sometimes folks think they aborted but it’s stuck in `ABORTING` because control-plane can’t write to routing policy store.

Mina Haddad: If stuck in `ABORTING`, paging control plane is critical. Routing will keep using last good config until policy store updates.

Ethan Park: Control-plane oncall angle: if abort action returns 5xx, do NOT retry blindly. Check if action is recorded first (list actions endpoint). Retrying with same idempotency key is ok, but use same key.

Rafael Mendes: Can we pin down the “abort vs hold” rule around missing telemetry?
I’d propose:
- If missing telemetry AND any customer reports/slack pings of impact => abort
- If missing telemetry AND no impact AND stage is <= 10% => hold
- If missing telemetry AND stage is 50%+ => abort (too risky to continue without signals)

Sean Gallagher: That rule seems reasonable.
Also “no promote during incident / high burn” — if error budget burn alert is firing, do not promote even if gates pass.

Peter Holtz: Love it. Adding.

Peter Holtz: Concrete commands/links to include? Oncall folks like copy/paste.

Ethan Park: For backend folks:
- Get rollout: `rwctl dedicated rollouts get --pool <pool_id> --plan <plan_id>`
- Abort: `rwctl dedicated rollouts abort --pool <pool_id> --plan <plan_id> --reason "<text>" --idempotency-key <uuid>`
- Hold: `rwctl dedicated rollouts hold ...`
And audit search: `rwctl audit search --resource dedicated_pool/<pool_id> --since 2h`

Chloe Martin: Console link pattern:
`https://console.redwoodinference.com/admin/dedicated/pools/<pool_id>/rollouts/<plan_id>`
Gate evidence is under “Stages” > select stage.

Allison Grant: Dashboard link pattern (stage view):
`https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/dedicated-rollout-stage?var-pool_id=<pool_id>&var-plan_id=<plan_id>&var-stage=<stage>`
(If it 404s you’re not on VPN.)

Logan Wright: If you suspect telemetry lag, check:
`https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/telemetry-ingest/lag?var-service=gate-evaluator`
If lag > 5m, assume signal failure and HOLD (unless customer impact).

Vanessa Ortiz: For capacity starvation/canary too small:
- Check “queue depth” panel + “active replicas per partition”. If canary has 1 replica and queue depth spikes, it’s not a model regression, it’s partition sizing.
Action: abort, then adjust stages (infra guideline).

Rafael Mendes: When you abort, please also capture:
- approximate time of first breach
- time abort executed
- time metrics recovered
We need that for MTTR.

Sean Gallagher: Also: open a ticket even if resolved quickly. We need a paper trail for beta.
- Customer impact => incident + postmortem
- No impact => INT ticket tagged `dedicated-rollouts`

Peter Holtz: Ok, I’ll consolidate into a Confluence runbook section, but keeping this thread as the source of truth for now. Any objections to paging order:
1) Abort/hold decision
2) Page `@sre-oncall` if impact or stuck state
3) Page routing/telemetry/fleet based on classification

Mina Haddad: Works.

Logan Wright: Works.

Irene Choi: Works. Add note: if GPU health gate fails, also check fleet maintenance window; sometimes we’re draining nodes and it’s expected.

Connor O'Brien: And if this is during a release train slot, drop a note in `#eng-releases` with plan_id/pool_id so we can correlate.

Peter Holtz: Got it. I’ll post the finalized checklist back here once updated.

source.messages:
Sean Gallagher: We need to nail down restore drill cadence + paging policy for Private. Right now the staging drill failures are noisy and folks are getting paged at 2am for non-prod :/

Sean Gallagher: Proposal: automated restore drill in staging 1x/week. Production drill cadence 1x/month (or quarterly?) but *not* automated at first. Thoughts?

Neha Kapoor: +1 on weekly staging. For prod, I’d start w/ quarterly until we have confidence + customer comms templates.

Rafael Mendes: Agree. Also: who owns responding when the drill fails? If we page “eng-oncall” then it needs to be actionable + have clear runbook.

Sergio Costa: From dashboards side, the metrics we’re emitting (`restore_drill_success`, `restore_drill_duration_seconds`, `restore_drill_last_success_ts`) are ready. We can alert on “no successful drill in N days” vs “single failure” to reduce flapping.

Allison Grant: +1. Alerting should be based on SLO semantics:
- staging: notify (Slack) on failure, page only if error budget burn / repeated failures
- prod: page on failure (since it implies DR readiness regression)

Talia Benson: Can we align to ENG-9827 (restore drill automation) + ENG-9826 (alerts) acceptance? PM asks for a concrete supportable policy.

Miles Thornton: Cadence suggestion by risk:
- Staging: weekly drill, but treat as canary for tooling
- Prod: monthly is ambitious; quarterly is fine IF you also do “backup integrity checks” daily
Also important: distinguish “control plane restore validation” vs “etcd snapshot restore” drill.

Benji Okafor: FWIW the drill currently fails if CRDs aren’t present / ordering issues (we saw this in the on-prem case). If we page on single staging failure we’ll be paging a lot until we harden the restore ordering.

Sean Gallagher: OK. So: staging weekly, but page only if it’s persistently failing (or no success for X days). Non-prod: gate pages to business hours.
